Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd spend hours going through material, usually late at night when everything was quiet. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create color-coded diagrams and mind maps from the lecture content. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything—it really depended on what else was going on in my life and how I was feeling mentally.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot based on deadlines and honestly, my stress levels. I couldn't stick to a regular schedule even when I tried. When assignments were due, I'd cram everything into a few days. The rest of the time, I might go a week without touching course materials, which is probably why my attendance was so low even though I was actually learning the content.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed since I wasn't attending classes regularly. If I saw peers posting in discussions, that might pull me in too, but honestly those moments were rare for me.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do. The only semi-routine thing was checking it Sunday nights to see what the week ahead looked like. Even then, I'd often download what I needed and work offline instead of staying logged in.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me as a visual learner—I watched almost 188 minutes worth and often rewatched sections multiple times. I also found the assignment rubrics incredibly helpful because they told me exactly what was expected. Anything with diagrams, charts, or visual explanations stuck with me way better than walls of text.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted twice the entire course. They felt time-consuming and I never felt like I had something valuable enough to add. The reading materials without visual components I'd often skim or skip entirely because they just didn't match how my brain processes information. I knew I should engage more but I just couldn't make myself do it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it helped me actually understand a concept I was struggling with. Visual resources, practice problems with feedback, anything that showed me immediately whether I understood correctly—those were valuable. If something felt like busywork or wouldn't impact my grade, I'd skip it without guilt.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. With medium stress levels and moderate anxiety, I was always calculating what would give me the best return for my limited energy. Required activities got done because they had to, but optional ones? I'd only do them if I was genuinely curious or if I had extra time, which was rare.
Q9: Oh absolutely, this was basically my main study method. I'd download lecture slides and videos, print out diagrams, and work from my own handwritten notes covered in highlighters and sticky notes. I had a whole system of visual summaries that I'd spread across my desk. My textbook was full of annotations. Most of my actual learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: Exam prep is the biggest example—I scored nearly 98% on my exams but that preparation was almost entirely offline. I'd be at my desk for hours with printed materials, creating flashcards and visual summaries, testing myself repeatedly. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My assignment scores were solid too, but the work behind them was invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, download the slides, and then review those slides probably five or six more times offline while adding my own notes. The LMS would only show that single view, but I was engaging with that content repeatedly. It was just more efficient for me to work with physical or downloaded materials.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety spiked and everything felt overwhelming. My attendance dropped because getting to class felt impossible some days. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me push through those moments, but there were weeks where I could barely look at course materials without feeling panicked.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or fatigued, the LMS felt like this overwhelming portal of obligations. I'd avoid logging in because just seeing the list of things to do made my anxiety worse. Competing responsibilities—work, personal stuff—meant the LMS was often the thing that got neglected first, even though the course itself mattered to me.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything. I'd technically be 'active' in the system but I wasn't learning. Those sessions were basically wasted time that probably showed up as engagement in the data.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they gave me anxiety—putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt vulnerable. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until the pressure became unbearable because the perfectionist part of me was afraid of doing it wrong. I knew it was self-sabotaging but the avoidance felt protective in the moment.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd have low activity for weeks, then intense bursts right before something was due. It wasn't healthy but it worked for my grades. The LMS became this place I'd frantically visit in the 48 hours before a deadline, download everything I needed, and then disappear again.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to keep pushing, especially when it validated that my unconventional approach was actually working. When I got strong marks back, it reinforced that I could succeed even without perfect attendance or constant LMS presence. Constructive criticism helped me focus my limited energy on what actually mattered for improvement.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that someone like me could succeed without being constantly present, which I'm grateful for. Having clear deadlines and well-organized modules meant I could plan my bursts of activity. But the structure also assumed regular engagement, which made me feel like I was always playing catch-up even when I was doing well.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement much easier for me—huge help. What made it harder was when participation or attendance was expected regularly, because my life and mental state just didn't allow for that consistency. Discussion requirements felt like barriers rather than learning opportunities given my anxiety around public contribution.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for something, trying to understand what's expected, or maybe anxious about missing information. They could be overwhelmed and unsure where to start, so they keep checking without actually producing work. Or they might be perfectionists who research extensively but struggle to commit anything to paper.
Q21: That's literally me. I had low LMS engagement but earned a Distinction because my real learning happened offline. I'd extract what I needed from the system efficiently and then do the deep work elsewhere. High performance doesn't require constant platform presence—it requires effective learning strategies, whatever form those take.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, time spent. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and everything that happens offline. They can't capture whether you actually understood something, how many times you reviewed downloaded materials, or the mental and emotional effort behind your work.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material and putting in the cognitive effort to learn it, regardless of where or how that happens. It's not about logging in constantly or posting in forums—it's about wrestling with concepts until they make sense. For me, engagement looked like late nights with printed notes and colored pens, not LMS activity.
Q24: My entire journey of managing anxiety and stress while still achieving at a high level is invisible in the data. The mindfulness practices that helped me push through difficult periods, the offline study sessions that actually produced my learning, the resilience it took to succeed despite irregular attendance—none of that shows up. The data sees my low engagement score but misses that I earned a Distinction through sheer determination and strategic effort.
